text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Bravo Paris, we had a great time - apart from the refreshments

TheParis Olympicsare over and the bunting has already begun to be brought down, to make way forthe Paralympicsdecorations.

We saw top-level sport and some unforgettable sights – and came across a few surprises along the way.

Here are our top takeaways:

The first morning of the athletics programme at the Stade de France featured qualifying for the men’s hammer throw, the women’s high jump and 100m, and three decathlon events.

The stadium was rammed. It was a stark contrast fromRio 2016, when the athletics stadium was desolate during morning sessions.

Tokyo was even worse, but that was because of Covid. Every venue thatiwent to was packed, from swimming to canoe slalom.

Just before 9pm on 2 August, the entrants in the third heat of the men’s decathlon were at the starting blocks. But they could not hear the starter for the noise in the stadium.

The weird thing was, there was nothing else going on at the Stade de France. The cacophony grew louder, before it erupted in a huge cheer – the announcer calling for quiet was completely ignored.

The reason? Because just under 10 miles away at La Defense Arena,Leon Marchandhad just won the 200m individual medley in the pool – fans at the Stade de France had been watching on their phones.

There was a similar outburst of applause and cheering on the train into the Stade de France on 3 August when Teddy Riner, the French heavyweight judoka who helped light the Olympic cauldron at the opening ceremony, won his second gold medal at Paris 2024 and his fifth overall.

France demonstrated a massive dose of hubris when it announced that the opening ceremony would take in the Seine and the Parisian sunset.

It absolutely hammered it down for the entire ceremony, which seemed to take hours to get to the point.

French metal band Gojira playing a revolutionary anthem in the windows of the Conciergerie Palace was a nice touch, though.

Whether it was GB’s Andy Macdonald’s score in the skateboarding park event after an impressive (but “actually quite easy”, according to the man himself) backflip, or a home hope being judged as the worst in the field after literally making a splash in the diving, the French crowd were more than happy to let their feelings known with a deep and booming boo.

Since Simone Biles walked out of the team gymnastics competition at Tokyo 2020 citing the twisties – a mental block where gymnasts cannot decipher where they are in the air – there had been a huge question over whether she could win gold in Paris. She did. Three times.

And afterwards, she was extremely generous of her time, even answering questions like “what would you say to all the little girls watching” eloquently and with passion.

The Netherlands picked convicted child rapist Steven van de Velde to play beach volleyball for them. There was outcry from around the world. But at the beach volleyball venue, Dutch journalists were incredulous at the amount of attention it garnered.

While the gathered media were waiting to speak to Van de Velde’s playing partner, the Dutch journalists smiled and asked the other writers why the world’s press were suddenly so interested in a beach volleyball pool-stage match between Italy and the Netherlands.

To them, the story was their disbelief that their men were being covered by people from Australia, Norway, Turkey, Brazil and the UK.

Table tennis is an excellent spectator sport – especially when it is played out in front of a packed, partisan crowd.

Fan Zhendong had his acolytes out in force in the men’s singles gold-medal match.

They had T-shirts bearing his name (and face) and football-style scarves with their hero’s name on it. And they were deafening every time Fan won a point.

It might have been the ornate venue – the Grand Palais – and the huge French interest in the sport, but the atmosphere for fencing was some of the best at the Games.

And as a spectacle, it is well worth watching, even if you don’t know what the heck is going on.

The day before the men’s triathlon, the Seine was deemed to be too full of bacteria and partially decomposed faecal matter to swim in.

It was postponed to the same day as the women’s race. Earlier, the athletes had not been able to practice and get a feel for the currents at all.

Similarly for the 10km marathon swim – the day before the event on the final Thursday, the swimmers were unable to enter the water, due to the levels of gunk. Both times, the river was miraculously clean (well, clean enough) the very next day.

If you like beige, expensive nourishment mostly involving wheat, cheese and ham (and occasionally egg), then Olympic venue kiosks are perfect for you.

It was scarcely better for athletes in the village, as they complained about a lack of vegetables and protein. Adam Peaty toldithat some had discovered worms in their fish.

The only athlete whoiwitnessed being remotely positive about the food was American gymnast Hezly Rivera, who said the pizza and hamburgers were “not bad”. She’s 16. Of course she’s going to talk up the pizza and hamburgers.

While GB hopes were swiftly skewered early in the women’s competition when Molly Caudery failed to make a jump, the men’s event was box-office.

And Armand Duplantis was the headline act, leaping to a gold medal then breaking first the Olympic, then world record.

It was on the same night as Keely Hodgkinson won gold in the women’s 800m. She had barely finished her victory lap when Duplantis broke his first record.

A totally unedifying episode unfolded in the boxing, when it transpired that two fighters, Imane Khelif of Algeria and Lin Yu-ting of Taiwan, had failed gender tests conducted by the Russian-run world federation, the IBA.

The International Olympic Committee has dismissed the IBA as a viable entity – and we only had the IBA’s word that the two fighters had failed gender tests. It became clear that the row was politically motivated by the IBA, who appeared to want to destabilise the IOC.

But that did not stop misinformed armchair experts jabbing their fingers at their phones in the race to post on social media that there were two men in the women’s competition. They only embarrassed themselves.

Few will forget watching skateboarders performing kick flip threes or five-ohs to fakie with the Eiffel Tower and the Place de La Concorde obelisk as a backdrop. Bravo Paris, we had a great time.

Impartial news + intelligent debate

All rights reserved. © 2026 Associated Newspapers Limited.
title: Awful food and French booing: 14 things I witnessed at the Paris Olympics
author: Matt Butler
description: Bravo Paris, we had a great time - apart from the refreshments
